items.mistake: Using the gradient as the direction of steepest descent without negating for minimization
items.fix: Minimize loss with θ ← θ − η∇L, not θ + η∇L, unless you intentionally maximize.
items.why: ∇L points uphill on the loss; adding it increases loss to first order.
items.example: If ∇L = [4, -2] and η = 0.1, a descent step subtracts [0.4, -0.2]; adding it would climb the loss locally.

items.mistake: Assuming gradient components are independent directions in parameter space
items.fix: Treat ∇f as one vector in ℝⁿ; components are coordinates in the chosen basis, not separate unrelated scalars.
items.why: Coupled parameters mean changing one weight affects loss through shared computation paths—partial derivatives already encode that.
items.example: In f(w₁, w₂) = (w₁ + w₂)², both ∂f/∂w₁ and ∂f/∂w₂ equal 2(w₁+w₂); they move together along the same composite direction.

items.mistake: Shape mismatch between parameter tensor and its gradient
items.fix: Ensure `.grad` has the same shape as the parameter; for matrix W, ∂L/∂W is matrix-shaped, not flattened arbitrarily.
items.why: Wrong reshaping silently corrupts updates in matrix layers and convolutions.
items.example: W.shape = (64, 128) requires w.grad.shape == (64, 128); flattening one but not the other breaks `w -= eta * w.grad`.
